framework: A path qualifies only when >=3 prominent, proven people in the field visibly share the capability, each with a resolving link — the pattern across standouts is the evidence, not market demand. Ranked by strength of that pattern. Curated editorial, not DOL-derived.

paths.what_it_is: Designing, machining, wiring, and programming complete robots or machines solo, then publishing the entire build process (mechanical CAD, electronics, and control code) for others to learn from.
paths.why_it_signals: It proves one person can own the whole mechatronics stack end to end, rather than a single narrow specialty, and that the work holds up under public scrutiny.

paths.what_it_is: Designing and publishing open-source actuators, motor drivers, or field-oriented-control firmware that other roboticists adopt as building blocks for their own machines.
paths.why_it_signals: Actuator and control-loop design is one of the hardest, most failure-prone parts of a robot; shipping an open-source version that strangers rely on demonstrates deep, verifiable control-systems mastery.

paths.what_it_is: Designing, building, and driving robots in public combat or competition arenas (BattleBots and similar) across many seasons, with a documented history of wins, iteration, and survivable engineering.
paths.why_it_signals: Competition robots face a public, unforgiving pass/fail test every match, so a sustained track record is hard evidence of real mechanical and electrical design skill under pressure, not just a resume line.
